title: Information Technology (Regulatory Provisions) (Cap. 9 Framework)
description: Malta's information technology regulatory framework comprises provisions drawn from multiple legislative instruments addressing cybersecurity, computer misuse, electronic evidence, and related matters under the general law and Criminal Code (Cap. 9). This framework governs the regulatory aspects of information technology use, establishing obligations and rights in the digital environment and providing the legal basis for addressing unauthorised access to information systems, electronic fraud, and related matters in a manner consistent with the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime and applicable EU law.

provisions.title: Section 1. Regulatory Framework Overview
provisions.content: Malta's information technology regulatory framework addresses computer misuse, electronic evidence, and related matters through provisions embedded across multiple legislative instruments including the Criminal Code (Cap. 9), the Computer Misuse Act, and applicable sectoral legislation. The framework is designed to be consistent with the Council of Europe Convention on Cybercrime (Budapest Convention), which Malta has ratified, and with applicable EU measures including Directive 2013/40/EU on attacks against information systems. The framework establishes the regulatory basis for addressing the security of information systems, the admissibility of electronic evidence, and the obligations of ICT system operators and administrators.

provisions.title: Section 2. Definitions for IT Regulatory Purposes
provisions.content: For the purposes of Malta's information technology regulatory framework, 'information system' means a device or group of inter-connected or related devices, one or more of which, pursuant to a programme, automatically processes computer data, as well as computer data stored, processed, retrieved, or transmitted by that device or group of devices; 'computer data' means any representation of facts, information, or concepts in a form suitable for processing in an information system; 'service provider' means any entity that provides to users of its service the ability to communicate by means of a computer system; 'traffic data' means any computer data relating to a communication by means of a computer system, generated by a computer system that formed a part in the chain of communication.

provisions.title: Section 3. Scope of Computer Misuse Provisions
provisions.content: The provisions addressing computer misuse in Malta's legal framework apply to conduct occurring within the territory of Malta, to conduct affecting information systems located within Malta's territory, and to conduct by Maltese nationals outside the territory of Malta where the conduct constitutes an offence under applicable law. The framework establishes categories of prohibited conduct in relation to information systems and computer data, addressing matters including unauthorised access, interference with systems and data, and misuse of devices. Provisions are applied in a manner consistent with the principle of proportionality and with the need to balance the protection of information systems with the legitimate interests of researchers, security professionals, and authorised testers.

provisions.title: Section 4. Unauthorised Access Framework
provisions.content: Malta's legal framework establishes the regulatory basis for addressing access to information systems or parts of information systems without right. The framework applies to access obtained by infringing security measures, by use of another person's credentials without authority, or through other means not authorised by the system owner or operator. Authorised security testing, penetration testing conducted under contract, and vulnerability research conducted in good faith under responsible disclosure practices do not constitute unauthorised access where conducted within the scope of the relevant authorisation. The legal framework requires that system owners take reasonable measures to secure their systems and establishes obligations relating to the disclosure and remediation of identified vulnerabilities.

provisions.title: Section 5. System and Data Interference
provisions.content: Malta's information technology regulatory framework addresses interference with information systems and computer data, establishing legal bases for addressing conduct that seriously hinders or interrupts the functioning of an information system by inputting, transmitting, damaging, deleting, deteriorating, altering, or suppressing computer data. The framework applies to intentional interference causing material disruption to systems used by public authorities, critical infrastructure operators, or users of electronic services. Conduct constituting legitimate network management activities, authorised testing, or maintenance operations within the scope of applicable authorisations does not constitute system interference under the framework.

provisions.title: Section 6. Electronic Evidence Admissibility
provisions.content: Electronic evidence, including computer data, electronic communications records, system logs, and digital forensic evidence, shall be admissible in legal proceedings in Malta subject to compliance with applicable rules on authentication, integrity, and chain of custody. Courts shall apply standards for the authentication of electronic evidence consistent with generally accepted digital forensic practices and with applicable international standards. Electronic evidence obtained in other jurisdictions may be admitted in Maltese proceedings subject to applicable mutual legal assistance treaty arrangements and to requirements of legality and proportionality. The weight to be given to electronic evidence shall be assessed by the court in the light of the circumstances of its collection, storage, and presentation.

provisions.title: Section 7. Obligations of Service Providers in Relation to Investigations
provisions.content: Service providers in Malta may be required by competent judicial or administrative authorities to retain and provide specified categories of data, including traffic data and subscriber information, for the purposes of investigations into offences addressed by the information technology regulatory framework, subject to applicable legal basis requirements, proportionality considerations, and data protection requirements. The obligation to retain data shall not extend to the content of communications without a lawful basis and appropriate judicial authorisation. Service providers shall establish adequate technical and operational capabilities to respond to lawful requests for data in a timely manner and shall designate a contact point for the receipt of such requests.

provisions.title: Section 8. Cybersecurity Responsibilities of ICT System Operators
provisions.content: Operators of information and communications technology systems in Malta shall implement security measures appropriate to the nature and sensitivity of the data processed and the systems operated, taking into account the state of the art and applicable industry standards. Operators of systems processing personal data shall comply with applicable data protection requirements including the security obligations under the GDPR. Operators of systems classified as critical information infrastructure shall comply with applicable cybersecurity requirements under the NIS Regulations and related legislation. MITA shall provide guidance on minimum cybersecurity standards applicable to ICT system operators in the public sector.

provisions.title: Section 9. Electronic Identity and Authentication
provisions.content: The use of electronic identification means and trust services in Malta is governed by Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) and applicable national implementing measures. Public sector bodies shall accept electronic identification means notified under eIDAS at the assurance level appropriate to the online service concerned and shall accept qualified electronic signatures and seals for document submission and authentication. The Malta Communications Authority, designated as Malta's eIDAS supervisory body, maintains the national trusted list of qualified trust service providers and oversees compliance with applicable requirements. National electronic identification schemes shall comply with the assurance level requirements established under eIDAS and applicable implementing acts.

provisions.title: Section 10. Data Breach Notification
provisions.content: Controllers subject to the GDPR shall notify the Information and Data Protection Commissioner without undue delay and, where feasible, within seventy-two hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach, unless the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. Providers of publicly available electronic communications services shall notify the Malta Communications Authority and MALTACERT of security breaches in accordance with applicable requirements. Critical infrastructure operators shall notify MITA of significant cybersecurity incidents in accordance with the NIS Regulations. MITA shall maintain a cross-regulatory view of significant incidents and shall coordinate responses where multiple regulatory frameworks are engaged.

provisions.title: Section 11. International Cooperation on Cybercrime
provisions.content: Malta cooperates with other states in the investigation and prosecution of computer-related offences through applicable mutual legal assistance treaty arrangements, including the framework established by the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime. The Attorney General's office and the Malta Police coordinate Malta's participation in international cooperation mechanisms for addressing cybercrime, including the Council of Europe's T-CY Committee, Interpol's cybercrime division, and Europol's European Cybercrime Centre. Malta participates in the EU Cybercrime Task Force and in Eurojust coordination activities in relation to serious cross-border cybercrime. Information shared under international cooperation arrangements is subject to applicable confidentiality requirements.

provisions.title: Section 12. ICT Procurement and Supply Chain Security
provisions.content: Public authorities procuring information and communications technology products and services shall incorporate security requirements into procurement specifications and contractual arrangements, in accordance with guidance issued by MITA. Procurement of high-risk ICT products and services shall be subject to enhanced security assessment requirements, taking into account applicable Union guidance on cybersecurity in procurement and relevant threat assessments. Public authorities shall assess the supply chain security risks associated with significant ICT procurements and shall document their assessment and the mitigating measures implemented. MITA shall issue and regularly update guidance on ICT procurement security requirements applicable to the Maltese public sector.

provisions.title: Section 13. Open Source Software
provisions.content: The regulatory framework does not impose restrictions on the use of open source software by organisations in Malta, and the use of open source software in the public sector is encouraged where it contributes to efficiency, interoperability, and transparency. Public authorities using open source software shall implement appropriate security practices, including vulnerability management and patch management, and shall contribute to the security of open source components used in public sector systems through responsible disclosure and upstream contribution practices where feasible. MITA shall maintain guidance on the security assessment and management of open source software used in the Maltese public sector.

provisions.title: Section 14. Cloud Computing
provisions.content: The use of cloud computing services by organisations in Malta is governed by applicable data protection, security, and sector-specific regulatory requirements. Public authorities using cloud computing services shall ensure compliance with applicable public procurement rules, data sovereignty requirements, and security standards established by MITA. Cloud service providers processing government data shall be subject to security assessment requirements and shall comply with applicable contractual standards. The regulatory framework is consistent with applicable Union guidance on cloud computing, including the ENISA cloud security guidelines and the EUCS cloud security certification scheme developed under the EU Cybersecurity Act.

provisions.title: Section 15. Coordination with European Regulatory Bodies
provisions.content: Malta's information technology regulatory authorities, including MITA, the Malta Communications Authority, and the Information and Data Protection Commissioner, shall cooperate with their counterparts in other Member States and with Union regulatory bodies including ENISA, EDPB, and BEREC in the development and implementation of consistent regulatory standards for information technology. Malta shall participate in relevant Union coordination mechanisms and shall contribute to Union-level consultations on draft legislation, standards, and guidelines relating to information technology regulation. Regulatory authorities shall maintain bilateral cooperation arrangements with the competent authorities of other Member States in areas of shared regulatory responsibility.

provisions.title: Section 16. Digital Literacy and Cyber Awareness
provisions.content: MITA, in cooperation with the Ministry of Education, shall promote digital literacy and cybersecurity awareness among citizens, businesses, and public sector employees in Malta. National cybersecurity awareness campaigns shall be coordinated with the European Cybersecurity Month programme organised by ENISA and shall address threats relevant to the Maltese context. Educational programmes for primary and secondary school students shall include digital literacy components covering safe and responsible use of information technology. MITA shall cooperate with the private sector in delivering cybersecurity training for employees and shall publish regularly updated guidance on common cybersecurity threats and protective measures for citizens and small businesses.

definitions.definition: A device or group of inter-connected or related devices, one or more of which, pursuant to a programme, automatically processes computer data, as well as computer data stored, processed, retrieved, or transmitted by that device or group of devices.
Any representation of facts, information, or concepts in a form suitable for processing in an information system, including a programme suitable to cause an information system to perform a function.

Any computer data relating to a communication by means of a computer system, generated by a computer system that formed a part in the chain of communication, indicating the communication's origin, destination, route, time, date, size, duration, or type of underlying service.
